data.title: Cal-Maine Foods: Cheap Enough To Begin Accumulation
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Dislocation Capital as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Cal-Maine Foods (CALM) is the largest producer and distributor of fresh shell eggs in the U.S. CALM has 14% market share in the fresh shell egg market, and during FY 2025 (June 1, 2024, to May 30, 2025), it generated $4.3 billion in net revenues. The company maintains a highly integrated supply chain by closely managing everything from breeding the hens and producing feed to hatching eggs, processing, packaging, and nationwide distribution.

CALM maintains a variable dividend policy of paying out a third of its quarterly net income to shareholders. If the company reports a loss, no dividend is paid, and CALM must become cumulatively profitable again before resuming payouts.

The business has been discussed extensively on Seeking Alpha, and I believe that there's an opportunity to start allocating capital to CALM at around ~$87. The focus of this article is to define a framework for the value we can get by purchasing CALM now. In my mind, CALM is a company providing a variable and volatile income stream while slightly reducing its earnings volatility over time with an improvement in sales mix. I do not think it can be valued using simple ratios since we are close to the peak of the commodity cycle and there are no clear competitors to compare with.

Instead, I focus on what the current share price tells us about expectations for CALM's long-term free cash flow, thereby accounting for the highly cyclical nature of this company. Rather than forecasting highly variable future outcomes, I use a reverse DCF framework to identify the cash flow expectations of the market (indicated by the stock price) and then assess if I believe the expectation is realistic based on industry- and company-specific factors. I call this a belief-based valuation framework. First, let's start with the most important variable driving CALM's valuation - Egg prices.

The Importance of Price

At its core, CALM continues to be a commodity-driven business. Commodity (EGG) prices are the primary indicators of revenue and, in turn, stock prices and free cash flow (FCF). CALM traded in a relatively well-defined range till 2022, reflecting the mean-reverting nature of commodity producers. The increase in per-share value over the last few years coincides with the rapid increase in wholesale egg prices - a theme largely discussed in the media over the past year.

Looking beyond stock prices, there seems to be a similar relationship when comparing the average retail egg prices with the FCF generated by CALM within the same periods.

Note: Capital expenditures play a role in the conversion of net income to FCF, but I have taken the reported values as directional estimates to visualize the underlying economics, rather than try to model this relationship.

The price of eggs currently shows us that the cycle has possibly peaked. Let's move on to assessing what the market is pricing in for the trajectory of FCF.

Market Expectation

Using the aforementioned belief-based valuation framework, I start off by identifying the embedded expectations for long-term FCF growth in the current stock price across multiple historical FCF values (peak, mid-cycle, and trough). Assume a 10% discount rate and assume a 2% terminal growth rate (the Federal Reserve's long-term expectation for inflation) after the first 10 years across all scenarios. I do not expect to see the FCF lows seen in 2021 or 2022 due to the broad-based inflation across all products. So I have not used FCF values from those years.

These results highlight that the market does not expect continued growth in FCF from the peak of $841M seen in 2025. The market is pricing a 12% yearly decline for the next 10 years, indicating a drop to around $234M by the 10th year. Reality, however, may not be as smooth. The drops and increases in FCF may be more pronounced and highly impact DCF calculations since cash flow in the present is more valuable than cash flow in the future - drastic drops that appear in the near future can lead to a meaningful change in these numbers. Using the 2024 $226M as a worst-case scenario for the 2026 fiscal year, FCF would need to grow at around 7% for 10 years to justify this valuation.

Given the trajectory of current egg prices (currently declined to $3.50 as of September 2025), I assume that the average egg prices in fiscal 2026 could stay above the $2.43 seen in 2024. Elevated egg prices, along with the fact that CALM has already reported $80M in FCF (almost double compared to the $40M in the same quarter of 2024) in the last quarter, possibly indicate that the actual FCF will come in higher than the $226M for fiscal 2026. Anything above that number means the market is expecting a growth rate of less than 7% going forward from fiscal 2026. Rather than trying to forecast what those future numbers could be, let's focus on the reasons why egg prices may be structurally higher going forward, thereby driving more growth than the market expects.

Demand Stability

Egg sales appeared to be price inelastic during the spike up in prices in the last few years, as evidenced by the chart showing CALM's egg sales over time.

CALM estimates that 97% of the population consume eggs, with volume growth supported by the growth in population over time. They also show that eggs are the cheapest form of protein - possibly providing an economic tailwind as beef prices have risen and are expected to continue rising in the near future. Health-conscious consumers may also be motivated by the FDA's December 2024 declaration that eggs meet the 'healthy' definition. CALM expects this to also boost consumer demand; however, the impact remains to be seen. Source.

Volume inelastic goods are more impacted by supply shocks, which are the drivers of sudden price volatility, since demand does not ease off during bad times. Let's focus on the supply side of the equation next.

Handling supply shocks

I prefer companies that accept and prepare for their cyclical nature rather than act like growth is linear. CALM seems to have embraced this by openly accepting the cyclical nature of the egg industry and planning for it. Having no debt and the variable dividend policy that protects capital during downturns are a great way to build resilience and reduce risk. There are other ways management has shown their prowess at handling supply shocks. Examples below

Since 2014-2015, management has invested over $75M into biosecurity to protect their operations from the recurring HPAI (highly pathogenic avian influenza) supply shocks impacting the industry, possibly providing it with more staying capability vs. smaller producers. This has been a recurring and large-scale problem for the industry, as evidenced by the numbers below.

CALM's scale and integrated operating model allowed it to continue servicing customers even when production has sometimes been halted at their facilities due to HPAI. When some of their Kansas and Texas facilities were impacted, the company increased their outside egg purchases to offset lost production and meet contractual commitments. While the approach to using outside purchases can be more expensive in the short term, it may help companies like CALM to strengthen customer relationships and possibly retain and/or grow market share during periods of turmoil. As conditions get better, it provides the company with an opportunity to retain this share while subsequently replacing outside purchases with more efficient self-production. This is an advantage provided by the scale of the company. The company has meaningfully reduced its reliance on outside eggs over the years. Source

Having more internal production can result in better cost control. This can also be a tailwind for CALM's FCF.

Beyond supply and demand, increased regulation can be a driver of structural increases in pricing. Let's cover its impact on cost concerns and consolidation next.

Increasing regulation and consolidation

Egg production is expected to incur increasing regulation costs over time, with over 10 states passing cage-free legislation or regulation for implementation by 2030. CALM has estimated that 27% of households will be in cage-free states by then. As specialty eggs grow to be a higher % of sales, there may be less fluctuation in prices since CALM (and possibly other competitors) does not use market-based pricing for the majority of their specialty eggs. (Source: FY2024 10-K). Conventional eggs appear to contribute more to the volatility of prices.

Though slightly dated (Feb 2023), there is research that indicates that companies are facing increased costs to satisfy retailer expectations on meeting cage-free requirements. This shows that increased spending may not lead to a proportional increase in revenue, as the 8% higher revenue of selling cage-free is expected to be offset by costs that are 8%-19% higher on average. Larger producers may be able to use their economies of scale to offset these costs better than smaller producers. The challenges to obtain financing to convert also disproportionately hurt smaller producers. This has led to concerns of consolidation, given that larger producers often have better access to capital. Source: Caputo-et-al.-2023-February-20

Increased regulations costs and increased consolidation can be structural drivers of higher base egg prices going forward, while the increasing proportion of specialty eggs can reduce the volatility in prices due to the way they are priced. CALM has already committed capital to this transition and has a strong balance sheet, bolstered by $252 million in cash, and no long-term debt, to support future spending. CALM has also historically shown prowess at using M&A efficiently to grow their footprint by acquiring or contracting smaller players. This can be an advantage if the increased costs lead to opportunities for distressed M&A. Source.

Moving down the value chain

Historically, CALM has focused its M&A on consolidating its operations and realizing economies of scale. It has recently acquired Echo Lake Foods, which can drive the margin profile higher and increase the stability of their prices over time by increasing exposure to retail egg prices and adding additional product categories like ready-to-eat breakfast items (waffles, eggs, and pancakes).

CALM is majorly focused on supplying eggs for private label brands, with self-branded eggs making up only 18% of their shell eggs revenue. The acquisition of Echo Lake Foods has increased their exposure to branded specialty products and thereby exposure to retail pricing, which largely correlates with wholesale prices but has historically provided some stability during downturns in wholesale pricing. Note how during retail prices are more sticky, lag wholesale prices, and sometimes stay higher during downturns in wholesale prices (mostly seen in 2023). Source

The Echo Lake Foods acquisition also provides increased exposure to value-added breakfast egg offerings and alternate food categories (51% of Echo Lake Foods revenue comes from toast, waffles, & pancakes). This can help reduce the dependence of CALM on egg prices, while also possibly increasing the margin profile as CALM moves down the value chain. Some of these categories can be countercyclical to eggs and make CALM more comparable to a general food producer over time. Source.

Conclusion

Structural reasons like cage-free regulatory demands, increased industry consolidation, and recurring supply shocks in the demand-inelastic egg industry suggest that egg prices may normalize at a higher range than in prior cycles. While prices can continue to be volatile, this backdrop supports our view that participation in egg production could be more attractive than in the past. CALM provides a way to participate in this production while also benefiting from the diversification to other value-added categories. At current share prices, the market appears to be expecting low single-digit growth in FCF from a conservative starting point that's lower than today. Low expectations combined with possibly higher egg price floors and gradual improvement in the margin profile can also lead to CALM being viewed as a diversified food producer. Increases in margin profiles and reduced volatility in earnings can lead to multiple re-ratings as well.

Under this lens, I think CALM is a good business available at fair valuations. I also like the resilience CALM provides in bad times by having a bulletproof balance sheet, clever variable dividend policy, and adaptive management, and thus I have taken starter positions in CALM. Any further weakness in share price can be an opportunity for further accumulation.

data.title: The AI Bubble Is A Myth: Why I'm Concentrating Into Networking And Cooling
data.description:
I can't take it anymore. I just read another "AI is a bubble" article. This time by an open internet activist in my favorite financial paper, the Financial Times. I'll let you know I'm loaded up with AI stock, so while the indexes are slowly giving up a few basis points, my portfolio is being demolished. I viscerally understand why anyone would second-guess himself about an AI investment with what's been going on in this subset of the market.

My take on the AI and meme stock drawdown is that it is likely a confluence of things, but mostly liquidity, flow, and reflexivity related, rather than a fundamental break. AI being one of the better trades of the year attracts a lot of fast money. The government shutdown lasted quite long, which ran up the Treasury General Account and withdrew liquidity from the markets. Then the President threatened a 100% tariff on rare earths on October 10. Markets were closed, but crypto liquidity is always available, so traders started protecting themselves there. Crypto can have these terrible liquidity cascades with a lot of leverage being available, and I think there was likely some spillover into the AI ecosystem. I imagine a lot of portfolios holding one theme were also exposed to the other.

The Bank of Korea's Governor Rhee Chang-yong recently expressed doubt about Korean retail investors plowing into U.S. AI stocks. He was particularly worried by the viral nature of the idea to invest in the space. I agree that's generally worrisome, but don't really agree the publicly traded AI companies are in a huge bubble. Meanwhile, its home market surged some 80%:

Guess why? I'll get to this later.

Earnings and growth are great

If you ask me, virtually all the earnings calls and reports in the AI ecosystem and I've listened to many, many calls were somewhere between good to incredible. Many sold off on the event, usually attributed to expectations being very high. I won't deny that expectations were high. Also keep in mind that the growth rates tend to be so great that most of the value is tied to future production. Often, companies are waiting for their production facilities to get online before earnings can meaningfully step up. Increasing outlooks and promises only gets you so far and given the skeptical mood last quarter it didn't sound as convincing I guess.

I also think heavy call buying in these names played a role. As soon as the earnings event is over, the volatility is sucked out of these calls. This means market makers have to hold less equity to hedge these options and start selling. The holders of the calls see the value evaporate faster than the price action justifies and start selling too. This quickly sets off a vicious cycle which then gets interpreted in the media as another event of traders confirming doubts around AI earnings.

Model builders

The FT columnist specifically singled out the model builders, arguing that if open-source models can match closed models at a fraction of the cost, the "moat" collapses and valuations plummet. As I've said I'm broadly sympathetic to that idea, but I don't think the market dominating narrative is priced in at all. OpenAI attracted something like 800 million users. Its product is at least an interesting competitor to Alphabet's search product. Alphabet being valued at multi-trillions of dollars indicates there's probably a way to create value for shareholders at the OpenAI level. Last capital raise OpenAI was valued at ~$500 billion. As far as I understand the company is growing revenue faster than any company has ever done before.

What seems most likely to me now is that there will only be a few closed models good enough to interact with regularly. This "interface" layer will have access to data to understand your needs better. I expect it will interact with other models and agents, choosing the best value for the task. I don't see great advantages to open-source at this interface layer yet because the technologies haven't really forked in different directions. The closed researchers can implement the novel features figured out by the open community. Meanwhile, several of the model builders also own vast cloud infrastructure at scale. They have the insight into what workloads look like and can utilize resources much more efficiently.

Long term they may hold a cost advantage over open source. I don't think there will be so few that we'll be faced with monopoly-like rents. In addition, OpenAI and Alphabet both have missions that aren't aligned with maximizing margins on inference. It looks to me like Alphabet is actively pursuing a strategy of enormous volume at relatively low margins. Low inference costs with brilliant models, fed signals and data by highly specialized cheap models, opens up a market that is hard to imagine.

The valuation of modelbuilders could be too high. But I can't invest in OpenAI anyway, except indirectly through SoftBank (SFBTY). Meanwhile, I'm bullish on a lot of companies in the AI supply chain based on the empirical observation that most of these AI models improve when more compute is used to train them. The required compute is exponential and semiconductors are not delivering increased performance anywhere near that level. If you think it's a useful technology there's only one way forward and it means buying exponentially more computers.

This leads me to the conclusion that there will likely only be a few companies really trying to take the top spot. The leading model builders are spending around $500 million on the final training run of a top-tier model. It could be a bit less in Alphabet's case because it managed to train Gemini-3 on its own ASICs and has extremely efficient infrastructure. For most players training is about to get oppressively expensive. I don't think it will be as expensive as simple extrapolation would suggest, but costs have risen where they are starting to be seriously weighted against speed. This is one of the reasons I think Alphabet is in a very good position because it has been playing the long game with a focus on efficiency for a long time.

The current AI technology isn't at the level of "press a button and all your problems are solved," but that seems like a high bar. Overall, I'm very impressed with the coding tools, the vibe coding tools, agentic environments like Antigravity and lots of interesting applications like Google LLM notebook. It is very hard to imagine businesses not finding ways to increase their productivity when every one of their employees is able to do stuff you previously needed engineers to enable and maintain.

Bubble valuations

If we look at valuations, we need to put it in context. The S&P 500 (SPY) is trading at 27.7x which is expensive vs historical norms. The true expensiveness is likely understating what's really going on under the hood because the 10% most expensive stocks are often entirely disregarded in standard metrics(to avoid skewing the average with a commodity producer in a downturn trading at 1000x earnings). I don't view Tesla (TSLA) as a true AI stock. It trades at over 300x earnings and it's a substantial weight in the index.

Palantir (PLTR) is often cited as the poster child of overvaluation, trading at 400-500x earnings. The valuation is challenging, but I think the "bubble" label there is overblown. It looks like a very attractive business to me with potentially a wide moat. Palantir wasn't doing all that great until they discovered the "bootcamp" as a sales technique. When they got people to start bringing their own data to demonstrate what practical possibilities this unlocked, sales started accelerating. Palantir specifically takes care of enterprise problems like data access, privacy concerns, and audit trails. Its CEO, Karp, actually believes this is the hard part and the LLMs are interchangeable.

At the heart of the AI boom is Nvidia, which is the leading supplier of the "AI-factory." Right now it trades at only 38x earnings and just 20x forward earnings. That's below the average for the S&P 500. AMD is trading at a much higher multiple, but I believe its revenue and earnings will explode in 2027. There is increasing evidence the CUDA moat is deteriorating which enables AMD to get some share.

Broadcom (AVGO) traded at a more challenging valuation but got trashed last week and now it looks very reasonable to me. It has major other business segments that hide the explosive revenue growth from the ASIC and networking business related to the AI-boom. Sure, it trades a tad above the artificially deflated S&P 500 earnings multiple but its expected growth rate is around 34% (which I think it will outperform) versus an expected 15% for the broader index.

Memory boom

Two months ago I wrote an article titled "Breaking the Cycle: How AI Transforms Memory Stock Micron(MU)." It is actually about the memory business at large. I own all the three big players, including the two from South Korea. SK Hynix has grown 45% over the past year and it is just the beginning. There are a lot of signs workloads are increasingly memory hungry. If the demand for GPUs doubles every year, the demand for memory probably grows even faster.

Micron reported December 18 and went up 10%. In the prepared remarks it quantified the 2025 HBM Market at $35 Billion and the 2028 HBM Market at $100 Billion, a 40% CAGR. Hynix and Micron both increased EPS by 200% over the past year. They trade at roughly 8x to 10x earnings.

On the same day Micron reported, the House voted to pass The Speed Act to speed up the permitting process for AI infrastructure buildouts. Now, these companies are cyclical. Usually demand drops off quickly. It could be dangerous to buy 40% revenue growth at an 8x multiple. However, I've seen worse ideas. I've had much worse ideas.

Hyperscalers

Then there are the hyperscalers. In my view these are an incredible good position to profit of the global AI-rollout. I like Alphabet because its models are really great world models. It has an incredible cost advantage. Miraculously the original amazing search business wasn’t negatively affected and enables much more favorable financing of the required CapEx.

Margins for the cloud business are likely going down. Providing cloud capacity to pre-train and finetune large models is much worse business compared to the broad cloud. This is part of why Microsoft (MSFT) didn’t want to stay married to OpenAI. It requires much more expensive equipment that is overqualified for subsequent more pedestrian inference work. The latter has geographic constraints while the former doesn’t.

That’s the opportunity for Coreweave(CRWV) e.a. They are providing specialized infrastructure. But they can offer their compute to a number of modelbuilders. This is much more attractive than renting out a random H100. The training runs are 24/7 for months on end. How fast these chips deteriorate isn’t really an issue. The issue is there’s better chips coming out. The compute improves even (as software solutions increasingly utilize it better) but over time it becomes less competitive in terms of compute/watt. Is it feasible to replace chips but networking and cooling stays… That means overinvesting now but you’d potentially save money down the line. There’s some uncertainty to the business model. You end up with few customers with a lot of bargaining power. Your datacenters are overqualified for inference (but they can compete for it obviously). For some it could be a way to scale up a global cloud business which used to be dominated by hyperscalers and it is worth some suffering to get to that scale.

Back to the hyperscalers; Alphabet, Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META) and Amazon (AMZN) themselves. Sure these trade around the average of the S&P 500... If this is bubble territory, the S&P 500 is in a bubble?

If the AI doesn't work, they can just stop the spending and they remain the best businesses in the world. Compared to historical earnings most of them trade somewhat elevated because they're investing for growth...

The picks and shovels

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) is the fab everyone needs to get their chips printed. It trades at 22x earnings and 27x forward earnings. Why does it trade at such high forward multiples? Same reason many of the hyperscalers trade at elevated multiples. If we want compute it needs to buy ASML's (ASML) machines and build factories to house them. TSM has been reticent because these machines last 30 years and it is unknown whether the scaling laws will last or be pursued. ASML trades at 36x earnings. Just like Nvidia it is barred from selling advanced equipment to China. TSM is doing everything it can to avoid buying its most advanced machines (which are double the price). I think that will change as the desire to improve energy efficiency increases and TSM realizes its customers need it to advance nodes and will pay.

Because TSM has been slow to add capacity this gives an opening to Intel (INTC) and Samsung (SSNLF) to try and make a play at its foundry business. Chipmakers are desperate enough for capacity they're willing to roll the dice. Intel trades at a bubble valuation of 100x earnings but it is investing heavily. On price to sales metrics it trades at 3x which isn't that high. Samsung, also investing in foundry and other equipment, trades at 23x earnings. It also trades at 8.7x free cash flow. Samsung and SK Hynix together make up around 40% of the Korean index so maybe its retail investors aren't so crazy to diversify.

Conclusion

I think I went over the vast majority of the market cap of public AI companies. There are some dumb valuations in the private markets, but by and large the public side looks very reasonable to me. At least as reasonable as the S&P 500 in general. In my view, most of these companies are somewhere between highly undervalued (memory), I guess because there's so much bubble talk, and moderately overvalued. The scaling "law" not holding up is one of the biggest risks. If we're going into an S-curve right now where adding compute to training runs doesn't translate into meaningful results, some of these deserve to get rerated. But the recent release of Gemini 3 implies scaling is still on. Although the bubble narrative appears overblown the big names aren't necessarily the best values in AI. The smaller companies are most interesting for 2026 especially in networking and cooling. I expect we're coming to an inflection point where the content of networking and cooling in the "AI-factory" goes up tremendously. Even outpacing memory.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Northern Ultra-Short Fixed Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
It was an atypical macro backdrop, as economic activity defied expectations for a slowdown despite clear signs of labor market deceleration. Following several upward revisions, U.S. second-quarter real GDP growth—initially reported in July—was finalized at 3.8% annualized, well above the original consensus estimate of 2.6% annualized. Third-quarter growth projections were revised higher, with some forecasts now anticipating another period of above-trend expansion. This underlying strength was fueled by robust capital investment and resilient consumer spending. However, U.S. job growth slowed to levels rarely observed outside of recessionary periods. A constrained labor supply helped prevent a sharp rise in the unemployment rate, but the moderation in employment was sufficient to prompt the U.S. Federal Reserve's first rate cut of 2025—marking the beginning of what markets expect to be an easing cycle through most of 2026. On the inflation front, tariff-related passthrough effects have remained manageable so far. This has allowed the U.S. Federal Reserve to shift its focus more squarely toward supporting growth, even as goods inflation continues to exert upward pressure on core inflation. Economic activity outside the U.S. also showed resilience. Key European business surveys improved, and Germany advanced plans for fiscal support. Most non-U.S. central banks continued easing policy, contributing to a more accommodative global stance. While risks remain, global recession fears stemming from weak trade dynamics eased as the initial tariff shock faded and major trade deals were announced.

Major asset class returns were largely positive, with outperformance in equities relative to fixed income. Fixed income returns were around 2% across both investment grade and high yield—helped by some tailwinds from both rates and credit. Treasury yields declined modestly while credit spreads tightened and remain near 2025 lows. The U.S. dollar was mostly unchanged in 3Q but remains down close to 10% since the start of 2025. In the real assets space, natural resources outperformed global equities, helped by metals & mining as gold was up about 16%. Global listed infrastructure and global real estate each gained roughly 4% but lagged global equities overall.

Treasury yields drifted lower, with the 2-year down 11 basis points (0.11%) to 3.61% and the 10-year falling 8 basis points (0.08%) to 4.15%—both near the bottom of their year-to-date trading range. After roughly a year-long pause, the U.S. Federal Reserve cut its policy rate in what Chair Powell called a “risk management” move, prompted by a shift in the outlook amid soft labor data. The resulting bull steepening in the curve directionally aligns with average historical patterns preceding U.S. Federal Reserve cuts. With 30-year yields slightly lower, the 5- to 30-year spread is near its widest since 2021.

Investment grade (IG) spreads tightened 9 basis points (0.09%) to 0.70%, while high yield (HY) spreads narrowed 23 basis points (0.23%) to 2.67%. From a return perspective, IG fixed income gained 2.0%, supported by lower rates, while high yield outperformed with a 2.5% return. All-in yields continued to fall, now around 4.5% for IG and 6.7% for HY. Lower-rated credits generally saw the most spread tightening, including over 70 basis points (0.70%) in CCC-rated corporates. Credit spreads briefly widened following weak July jobs data but later reversed amid rising expectations for rate cuts.

Explanation of Results

The Fund outperformed its benchmark by 25 basis points (0.25%) this quarter. The Fund's total return was 139 basis points (1.39%) vs its benchmark, the Merrill Lynch 1 Year U.S. Treasury Note Index*, which added 114 basis points (1.14%) of return for the quarter. Corporate bonds contributed 28 basis points (0.28%) of excess return due to sector allocation, particularly in the banking sector (0.12% added). Securitized bond holdings contributed 2.5 basis points (0.025%), due to the allocations to Asset Backed, Commercial Mortgage Backed, and Mortgage Backed securities.

Governmental regulation may change frequently and may have significant adverse consequences for companies in the financials sector, including effects not intended by such regulation. The impact of recent or future regulation on any individual financial company, the banking industry or on the sector as a whole cannot be predicted. The valuation of financial companies has been and continues to be subject to unprecedented volatility and may be influenced by unpredictable factors, including interest rate changes and sovereign debt default.

Foreign governments may impose limitations on foreigners' ownership of interests in local issuers, restrictions on the ability to repatriate assets, and may also impose taxes. Any of these events could cause the value of the Fund's investments to decline.

data.title: Fidelity Corporate Bond ETF Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Taxable Bond Market Review

U.S. taxable investment-grade bonds gained 2.03% in the third quarter, as measured by the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index. Over the three-month period, investor sentiment shifted from caution to relative optimism, as concerns about U.S. trade and fiscal policy cooled and fixed-income investors welcomed the resumption of policy interest-rate cuts by the U.S. Federal Reserve. Year to date, the Aggregate index has gained a robust 6.13%.

Following a strong June – when the index rose 1.54% – the third quarter got off to a sluggish start, with the bond market taking a small step back in July (-0.26%). Bond yields moved higher and credit spreads widened after data from the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics showed inflation accelerating in June. Headline inflation rose 2.7% year over year, up from 2.4% in May. The yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury, which began the quarter at 4.24%, hit a peak of 4.50% on July 15 before easing to 4.37% by month end. At its July 29-30 meeting, the Fed held rates steady for the fifth consecutive time, maintaining the pause on monetary easing that began in January.

On August 1, a new jobs report showed a sharp slowdown in hiring, with the U.S. economy adding just 73,000 jobs in July. Additionally, job gains for May and June were revised downward. Mid-month, the inflation rate held steady at 2.7%. Meanwhile, corporate earnings remained strong, and the Trump administration's newly implemented tariffs on U.S. trade partners proved less severe and disruptive than the market had initially feared. Taken together, these developments were seen as giving the Fed clearance to resume cutting the federal funds rate at its next meeting in September. The index advanced 1.20% in August.

U.S. Treasury Yield Curve

On September 17, the central bank lowered its benchmark policy rate by a quarter point (25 basis points), asserting its view that supporting a weakening labor market outweighed concerns about inflation running slightly above target. Bond yields declined and credit spreads tightened in anticipation of this decision, boosting price performance and helping the Aggregate Bond index gain 1.09% for the month. At quarter end, the market expected the Fed to implement additional quarter-point cuts at its two remaining meetings in 2025 before adopting a slower pace in 2026.

Over the quarter, longer-dated bonds with maturities of 10+ years posted the strongest advance at 3.26%, while intermediate and short-term issues lagged, with 7-10 year bonds gaining 2.58%, 5-7 year maturities up 1.82%, and 1-3 year issues rising 1.20%. By credit rating, bonds rated BAA (the Bloomberg equivalent to BBB) outperformed higher-quality issues.

Results at the sector level reflected a positive tone for risk assets, with yield-advantaged, credit-sensitive sectors broadly outperforming Treasuries (+1.51%). U.S. corporate bonds (+2.60%), buoyed by a strong earnings season, led among investment-grade categories, followed by government-agency mortgage-backed securities (+2.43%) and government-related bonds (+2.23%). Commercial mortgage-backed securities (+1.75%) and asset-backed securities (+1.64%) more narrowly outpaced Treasuries.

Outside the Aggregate index, U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities gained 2.10%, per Bloomberg, while U.S. high-yield corporate bonds (+2.54%) finished slightly below investment-grade corporates. Notably, emerging-markets debt delivered the bond market's best returns for the quarter – rising 3.58% for investment-grade and 3.09% for high-yield issues. ■

Performance Review

Detailed Fund Attribution Relative to Benchmark

Strategy: Sector Allocation
Market Environment Amid growing investor appetite for risk, yield-advantaged sectors broadly outperformed U.S. Treasurys. Among investment-grade categories, corporate bonds led the way.
Fund Positioning(Impact vs. Benchmark)

Sector allocation hampered performance versus the benchmark Bloomberg U.S. Credit Bond Index.

  • An out-of-benchmark stake in U.S. Treasurys – which the fund held, along with cash, for liquidity and hedging purposes – detracted from relative performance in Q3. (Negative)
  • Underweight exposure to other government-related debt, most notably bonds issued by Panama and Mexico, also hurt. (Negative)
Strategy: Security Selection
Market Environment By maturity, longer-dated (10+ years) bonds had the strongest gain. By credit quality, securities rated BBB outpaced higher-quality investment-grade bonds.
Fund Positioning(Impact vs. Benchmark)

Security selection contributed to the ETF's relative performance.

  • Within industrials, bond selection in consumer-related industries, as well as energy, technology and capital goods, helped. Standout holdings in these groups included Hess (HES), Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ:CA) and General Motors (GM). (PoSitive)
  • In financials, picks among banks, insurance companies and REITs – including JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Equitable (EQH) and Voya (VOYA) – added value. (PoSitive)
Strategy: Duration and Yield Curve
Market Environment Yields fell across the maturity spectrum, with declines sharpest at the front end of the curve.
Fund Positioning(Impact vs. Benchmark)

Yield-curve positioning – particularly in the intermediate-maturity portion of the curve – modestly detracted. (Negative)

Outlook and Positioning

We have a cautious outlook overall, given questions about how much the Fed will cut policy interest rates, the future independence of the central bank, reduced job growth and sticky inflation.

The tariffs implemented by the current administration triggered uncertainty. The question remains: When and to what extent do tariffs begin to affect the economy and what will that mean for corporate profit growth and consumer spending? In our view, tariffs will stoke inflation and dampen growth over time.

Despite these uncertainties, the average spread of the Bloomberg U.S. Credit Bond Index was near its tightest level in 20 years as of quarter end. As a result, we believe spreads may widen over the intermediate term. Whenever that occurs, we're in a position to capitalize on investment opportunities, given our reserve of U.S. Treasuries and cash. In the meantime, we'll continue our efforts to find bonds that we believe will provide some level of incremental return, while managing the overall level of credit risk in the portfolio.

During the quarter, we reduced the ETF's exposure to bonds issued by financial companies and redeployed some of this capital into the industrials and utilities sectors. We made this shift based on the view that hard-asset companies are likely to become more valuable in an economy that is placing greater emphasis on manufacturing.

Additionally, given the challenge of finding compelling values in the current spread environment, we added industrial and utility bonds that were trading toward the wider end of a tight range of credit spreads. In this way, we tried to locate issuers offering some measure of relative value.

As we rotated into these issuers, we kept the portfolio's overall risk profile stable, partly by maintaining a sizable allocation to Treasuries and, to a lesser extent, cash. As of September 30, about 8% of the portfolio was allocated to Treasuries and roughly 1% was in cash. With this positioning, we kept the fund's credit risk modestly greater than the benchmark, but, with spreads at a tight level, we did not extend it further.

Within financials, we continued to have overweight exposure to banks, emphasizing U.S.-based institutions. We increased our allocation to the highest-quality banks, especially the "Big 6" U.S. banks, which include names such as JPMorgan Chase and Bank of America (BAC).

From a credit-rating perspective, we continue to emphasize issuers rated BBB, believing that there is opportunity for alpha potential over time resulting from improving credit quality. At the same time, we maintained a relatively consistent allocation to bonds rated A, in response to tight spreads and economic uncertainty. ■

Fidelity Corporate Bond ETF (FCOR)

Investment Approach

Fidelity® Corporate Bond ETF is a credit-focused bond strategy that seeks a high level of current income.

Benchmarked against the Bloomberg U.S. Credit Bond Index, the fund seeks to deliver competitive risk-adjusted performance commensurate with investor expectations of a primarily investment-grade corporate bond fund.

Utilizing a team-based investment process, the fund relies on experienced portfolio managers, research analysts and traders. We concentrate on areas where we believe we can repeatedly add value, including security selection, industry allocation and opportunistic trading.

Robust governance and risk management – consisting of extensive quantitative modeling, formal and informal portfolio reviews, and proprietary tools – support the identification of both opportunities and risks.

Characteristics

Duration is a measure of a security's price sensitivity to changes in interest rates. Duration differs from maturity in that it considers a security's interest payments in addition to the amount of time until the security reaches maturity, and also takes into account certain maturity shortening features (e.g., demand features, interest rate resets, and call options) when applicable. Securities with longer durations generally tend to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than securities with shorter durations. A fund with a longer average duration generally can be expected to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than a fund with a shorter average duration.

30-day SEC restated yield is the fund's 30-day yield without applicable waivers or reimbursements, stated as of month-end.

30-day SEC yield is a standard yield calculation developed by the Securities and Exchange Commission for bond funds. The yield is calculated by dividing the net investment income per share earned during the 30-day period by the maximum offering price per share on the last day of the period. The yield figure reflects the dividends and interest earned during the 30-day period, after the deduction of the fund's expenses. It is sometimes referred to as "SEC 30-Day Yield" or "standardized yield".

Net Asset Value is the dollar value of one share of a fund; determined by taking the total assets of a fund, subtracting the total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding.

Fund Risks

In general the bond market, especially foreign markets, are volatile, and can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market, or economic developments. Fixed income securities carry interest rate risk. (As interest rates rise, bond prices usually fall, and vice versa. This effect is usually more pronounced for longer-term securities.) Fixed income securities also carry inflation risk and credit and default risks for both issuers and counterparties. Unlike individual bonds, most bond funds do not have a maturity date, so avoiding losses caused by price volatility by holding them until maturity is not possible. Lower-quality bonds can be more volatile and have greater risk of default than higher-

quality bonds. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency-exchange-rate, economic, and political risks. Investments in mortgage securities are subject to the risk that principal will be repaid prior to maturity. As a result, when interest rates decline, gains may be reduced, and when interest rates rise, losses may be greater. Leverage can increase market exposure and magnify investment risk. The fund generally expects to effect its creations and redemptions for cash rather than in-kind securities, and may recognize more capital gains and be less tax-efficient than if it were to redeem in-kind. An ETF may trade at a premium or discount to its Net Asset Value (NAV). There can be no assurance that an active trade market will be maintained and trading may be halted due to market conditions.

Important Fund Information

Relative positioning data presented in this commentary is based on the fund's primary benchmark (INDEX) unless a secondary benchmark is provided to assess performance.

Indices

It is not possible to invest directly in an index. All indices represented are unmanaged. All indices include reinvestment of dividends and interest income unless otherwise noted.

Bloomberg U.S. Credit Bond Index is a market-value-weighted index of investment-grade corporate fixed-rate debt issues with maturities of one year or more.

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index is a broad-based, market-value-weighted benchmark that measures the performance of the investment grade, U.S. dollar-denominated, fixed-rate taxable bond market. Sectors in the index include Treasuries, government-related and corporate securities, MBS (agency fixed-rate and hybrid ARM pass-throughs), ABS, and CMBS.

Bloomberg U.S. Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities (TIPS) Index (Series-L) is a market value-weighted index that measures the performance of inflation-protected securities issued by the U.S. Treasury.

S&P/LSTA Leveraged Performing Loan Index is a market value-weighted index designed to represent the performance of U.S. dollar-denominated institutional leveraged performing loan portfolios (excluding loans in payment default) using current market weightings, spreads and interest payments.

J.P. Morgan Emerging Markets Bond Index Global tracks total returns for the U.S. dollar-denominated debt instruments issued by Emerging Market sovereign and quasi-sovereign entities, such as Brady bonds, loans, and Eurobonds.

Fidelity does not review the Morningstar data and, for mutual fund performance, you should check the fund's current prospectus for the most up-to-date information concerning applicable loads, fees and expenses.

Performance Summary

Net Asset Value (NAV) Return represents the closing price of underlying securities at which the fund can create and redeem shares directly with the security. Market Return represents the price at which an investor can buy and sell a security in the secondary market. Since ETFs are bought and sold at prices set by the market – which can result in a premium or discount to NAV – the returns calculated using market price (market return) can differ from those calculated using NAV (NAV return).

3-Year Risk/Return Statistics

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (leSs) than 1 is more (leSs) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

Trading Information

Bid/Ask Spread

In the case of exchange-traded securities, market liquidity is managed by the market maker community. Market makers provide prices at which they will buy (the bid) and sell (the ask) securities. The difference between the bid and ask prices is the bid/ask spread. The bid/ask spread gives an investor a sense of the liquidity and the transaction costs associated with a security. Securities with wide bid/ask spreads tend to be less liquid and have higher cost of ownership. Those with tight bid/ask spreads tend to be more liquid and have a lower cost of ownership.

Premium/Discount

The exchange-quoted price of an ETF frequently differs from the ETF's underlying value. The difference in these values represents an ETF's discount or premium. In the case of a discount, the exchange-quoted share price is lower than the ETF's underlying value. An ETF is at a premium when the exchange-quoted price is greater than its underlying value. An ETF's discount or premium fluctuates over time and is influenced by such factors as the transaction costs of the underlying holdings, the cost of creation and redemption of shares, and the average daily volume. The magnitude and volatility of an ETF's premium/discount will affect an investor's holding period return on an ETF. If an investor buys shares at a discount and sells at a premium, they should outperform the NAV return of the ETF. On the other hand, if an investor buys at a premium and sells at a discount, they should underperform the NAV return of the ETF.

Yield Curve

The relationship at a given point in time between yields on a group of fixed-income securities with varying maturities - commonly, Treasury bills, notes, and bonds. The curve typically slopes upward since longer maturities normally have higher yields, although it can be flat or even inverted.

data.title: Virtus Newfleet Low Duration Core Plus Bond Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fixed Income Market Review

The third quarter of 2025 was marked by shifts in macroeconomic, geopolitical, fundamental, and supply and demand dynamics. Markets rebounded from the lows following the “Liberation Day” announcements as immediate economic impacts remained subdued. The final outcomes for tariffs and other policies remain unknown, as do their longer-term economic impacts. However, financial markets gained confidence over the quarter that the economy remained solid, inflation remained rangebound, and geopolitical risks showed early signs of easing.

Economic data in the U.S. continued to point to a resilient economy. Growth improved after a weak first half, with inflation staying contained and unemployment holding near historically low levels despite some downside surprises. This allowed the Federal Reserve (Fed) to resume easing policy with a 25-basis-point (bps) reduction at the September Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting. The Fed has acknowledged that while elements of its mandate are in conflict, it has chosen to focus on protecting the labor market in the near term. Global monetary policy remains desynchronized, with divergences among the European Central Bank, the Bank of England, and the Bank of Japan. Geopolitical developments were mixed during the third quarter, with volatility re-emerging in the Russia/Ukraine conflict following the Alaska summit in August, while the Middle East saw some signs of progress.

The continuing market rebound resulted in generally positive financial market performance in the quarter. Fixed income sector performance was also positive, with interest rates lower during the period. Most spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries, and the U.S. Treasury curve steepened slightly. The 2-year Treasury yield decreased by 11 bps, the 5-year Treasury yield decreased by 6 bps, the 10-year Treasury yield decreased by 8 bps, and the 30-year Treasury yield moved 4 bps lower.

Fund Performance Review

The Fund (Class I) returned 1.56% in the third quarter versus the ICE BofA 1-5 Year US Corporate & Government Bond Index return of 1.29%.

Positive Contributors

  • U.S. Treasury Securities: The Fund’s underweight to U.S. Treasuries had a positive impact, as most spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries and had positive excess returns.
  • High Yield (HY) Corporates: Allocation to the sector was positive.
  • Investment Grade (IG) Corporates Selection: Selection within the sector was positive.

Performance Detractors

  • Asset-Backed Securities Selection: Issue selection versus the benchmark was negative for the period.
  • Residential Mortgage-Backed Securities (RMBS): Allocation to RMBS over agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and selection within RMBS had a negative impact on performance.

Current Fund Strategy and Positioning

  • Increased exposure to MBS, U.S. Treasuries, and cash.
  • Reduced exposure to RMBS, commercial mortgage-backed securities, IG corporates, and bank loans.
  • In addition to changes to the Fund’s sector allocation during the quarter, we continue to optimize positioning within sectors based on our view of the best relative value.

Emerging Markets (EM) Debt and Non-U.S. Exposure: The J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified Index returned 4.75% in 3Q, with EM HY outperforming EM IG. EM corporate debt returned 3.18%. In 3Q, the U.S. imposed a 50% tariff on Brazilian exports, straining ties and pushing Brazil toward China. Former president Jair Bolsonaro was convicted for attempting a coup after his 2022 loss. In Argentina, President Javier Milei’s party suffered a major defeat to Peronists in Buenos Aires, sparking market volatility. Later, the U.S. pledged $20 billion in aid to support Milei’s reforms. In Ecuador, President Noboa ended diesel subsidies, triggering widespread protests as fuel prices surged.

IG Corporates: IG corporate spreads ended 3Q in the low 70s—the lowest levels since 1998—while yields moved lower by 18 bps to 4.81%. Despite lower spreads and yields, IG saw positive fund flows in every week of 3Q. Institutional buyers remain active as well. Though supply for the quarter was heavier than the five-year average, it was easily absorbed. Upgrades outpaced downgrades year-to-date, though at a slower pace than in recent years. BBBs are at their lowest percentage of the overall market in a decade. We are focused on defensive industries given tight valuations, with a preference for utilities, pipelines, and capital goods.

HY Corporates: The HY market, as represented by the Bloomberg High Yield 2% Issuer Capped Bond Index, gained 2.54% in 3Q. Credit spreads tightened by 23 bps to +267 bps. Stronger-than-expected economic growth, a dovish Fed pivot that produced a 25-bps rate cut in September, resilient corporate earnings, and significant inflows contributed to performance. Media outperformed due to anticipated deregulation and positive merger and acquisition developments. Chemicals and packaging underperformed due to weak global demand.

Securitized: The securitized markets had positive total and excess returns for 3Q. data.title: Western Alliance Bancorp: Preferred Shares Less Appealing
data.description:
Western Alliance Bancorporation (WAL) is a $91 billion bank holding company primarily serving the western U.S. states of California, Nevada, and Arizona. We acquired the company's preferred shares in 2023 during the banking panic, which depressed prices in the aftermath of the failure of Silicon Valley Bank. Western Alliance was one of the institutions, along with PacWest Bancorp and others, speculated as being at risk should the fear contagion extend beyond the handful of fundamentally flawed institutions that ultimately failed as a result of the momentary crisis.

However, while Western Alliance was not, in our view, as robust an institution as some of the other institutions we wrote about at the time (notably PacWest Bancorp, which was never at any material risk), largely since the company's mix of assets made it more difficult to reliably assess its financial and regulatory capital position should it be stressed by a panicked withdrawal of uninsured deposits. Specifically, since the company's available-for-sale and held-to-maturity securities alone would not cover a withdrawal of uninsured deposits, an assessment required greater assumptions about the nature of the company's non-treasury securities and potential loans that would be sold-and the regulatory capital requirements associated with each-in what would have been a worst-case scenario.

Nonetheless, our assessment was that Western Alliance's financial position was still far stronger than implied by the severe discounts applied by the market to the company's common and preferred shares, and we accumulated preferred shares at a steep discount to their stated redemption price (and thus also at a very high current dividend yield) as a result of our assessment.

The preferred shares have since recovered a substantial portion of their valuation over the intervening two and a half years while continuing to pay the expected preferred dividends, though the recovery in valuation was more gradual than other bank preferred issues. The more gradual recovery was a function of both the company's ongoing perception of being more exposed to market risks and the low initial fixed dividend rate on the preferred shares in the face of rising benchmark interest rates. The recovery in valuations has caused us to periodically revisit the expected forward return potential to determine if the preferred shares warranted an ongoing portfolio position. Our focus revolved around two core questions:

  • First, what is the likelihood that the preferred shares will appreciate further to close the gap between market valuation and the redemption valuation?

and

  • Second, what is the effective long-term return potential based on the most likely outcome of the impending redemption/reset rate process?

Our assessment is that the potential for further appreciation in the market quotation towards the redemption value is possible but limited, and the preferred shares are unlikely to reach the redemption value outside of a redemption of the preferred shares, which we consider a less likely scenario. In addition, given the reduced likelihood of a redemption next year, the longer term return potential is substandard relative to alternate opportunities due to the reset rate premium. We have thus selectively sold all our positions in Western Alliance's preferred shares in the last few months.

Summary of Preferred Shares

The Western Alliance Series A preferred stock (WAL.PR.A) is a series of non-cumulative reset rate preferred stock with a liquidation/redemption price of $25.00 per share. The preferred stock yields a fixed 4.25% of the stated redemption value, payable quarterly, until September 30, 2026, at which time the preferred shares may be redeemed at the option of the issuer or, if not redeemed, the dividend rate is reset every five years at a premium of 3.452% over the then applicable rate on the five-year U.S. Treasury bond. The dividend rate is fixed for the five-year period from the reset date until the next reset date five years hence, although the preferred shares are redeemable on any dividend date after the initial reset date.

In addition, the preferred shares are subject to potential redemption, as with many bank preferred shares, in the event that regulatory changes no longer permit the preferred shares to be included as a component of Tier 1 capital for regulatory purposes. The preferred, from a credit rating standpoint, was initially rated BBB (Kroll) and Ba1 (Moody's) though the Moody's rating has since been reduced to Ba3.

Comparable Preferred Shares

In comparison, WesBanco recently issued a new preferred security (the WesBanco Preferred Series "B" (WSBCO)) in part to redeem the company's Series "A" shares with a similar five-year fixed to reset rate provision with a similar premium over the five-year treasury rate of 3.795%, suggesting the Western Alliance reset premium of 3.452% is approximate to or even slightly below the current market premium. The new WesBanco shares were issued at an initial fixed rate of 7.375%. The WesBanco preferred trades at approximately $25.65 per share on a when-issued basis, implying a current yield of 7.2% and a yield to call of approximately 6.8%. The WesBanco preferred shares were rated BBB- (Kroll) and Ba2 (Moody's) upon issuance.

Wintrust Financial has also recently issued a new preferred security (the Wintrust Preferred Series "F" (WTFCN)) with a similar five-year fixed-to-reset rate provision with a similar premium over the five-year treasury rate of 3.878%, similarly suggesting the Western Alliance reset premium of 3.452% is approximate to or even slightly below the current market premium. The new Wintrust preferred shares were issued at an initial fixed rate of 7.875% through July 15, 2030. This preferred currently trades at approximately $26.50 per share, a premium over the redemption value, for a current yield of approximately 7.4% and a yield to call of approximately 6.3%. The Wintrust preferred was rated BB (Fitch) upon issuance.

In general, we consider Western Alliance, WesBanco, and Wintrust as generally similar institutions, although WesBanco is significantly smaller than the other two companies. Regardless, in terms of financial position, capital ratios, operating metrics, and asset quality, the institutions are broadly compatible, though we consider Western Alliance arguably marginally weaker despite having stronger regulatory capital ratios due to relative underperformance on operating metrics as reflected by the following summary table.

Probability of Redemption

A compelling aspect of the PacWest Preferred Series "A" (BANC.PR.F) shares and WesBanco Preferred Shares "A" shares, which we acquired at the same time as our Western Alliance preferred share position, were their relatively high premiums over the five-year treasury bond rate applicable upon the first rate reset event. The WesBanco Preferred Series "A" shares, for example, carried an unusually high 6.557% premium over the five-year treasury bond rate, virtually guaranteeing, in our view, that the preferred shares would be redeemed at par on the first available redemption date. This preferred issue was indeed redeemed by WesBanco, as we expected, in November 2025. The PacWest preferred shares (now Banc of California Preferred Series "F" due to a corporate action) carry a lower, though still relatively high, 4.82% premium over the five-year treasury bond rate, also making a redemption on the first possible redemption date in September 2027 highly probable.

In contrast, as noted above, the Western Alliance Series "A" preferred stock's 3.452% premium is somewhat below comparable outstanding or recently issued preferred shares, making the rationale for redemption, barring significantly higher five-year treasury bond rates, much less compelling. In addition to the WesBanco and Wintrust preferred shares noted above, Flagstar Bancorp's outstanding Series "A" preferred shares, which are subject to rate reset in March 2027 (though originally against three-month LIBOR and now expected to be relative to three-month SOFR upon the initial reset), carry a premium over the benchmark rate of 3.821%. Similarly, other relatively recently issued reset rate preferred shares carry premiums generally comparable to these issues, while non-reset rate bank preferred shares recently issued (such as those from First Busey (BUSE) and Live Oak (LOB)) carry fixed dividend rates in the 7.5% to 8.25% range.

As a result, due to the somewhat below-market reset rate risk premium, we consider it less likely that Western Alliance will redeem the preferred shares at redemption value once the company is able to do so in September 2026. Instead, we believe it is likely the company will elect to allow the preferred shares to remain outstanding, at least for an extended period of time, at a higher dividend rate fixed for the next five years so long as five-year treasury bond rates remain at or below the current quotation and those presently forecast based on one-year forward interest rates.

Forward Yield and Valuation

The probability of redemption being reduced, we evaluate whether there is the potential for significant closure of the gap between the current market valuation and the redemption value, will significantly boost yields to maturity or rate reset. Our view is that Western Alliance's preferred shares warrant an equal or perhaps slightly higher effective yield relative to the very similar preferred shares issued by WesBanco and Wintrust, along with a generally similar premium to the five-year treasury bond from a reset rate perspective.

Western Alliance's preferred reset rate premium being 35 to 45 basis points slower than the recently issued preferred shares from both WesBanco and Wintrust means the corresponding reset rate, once implemented, will also be somewhat lower than the corresponding reset rates for the other preferred shares and should result in a market valuation discount relative to peer preferred shares. The peer preferred shares being recently issued and thus having a longer term fixed rate than the Western Alliance preferred shares provides for the current market premium to redemption for the peer preferred issues.

Our projections suggest that, assuming a five-year treasury bond rate of approximately 3.5% at the time of reset, the Western Alliance preferred shares would trade near or slightly below the redemption price per share of $25.00. The expected range would be $23.75 to $24.50 based on the corresponding current yield and yield to maturity, including expectations of prevailing benchmark interest rates at reset. In this case, there would still be some capital appreciation potential in the preferred shares from the current quotation of approximately $22.75, depending on the day, which, when added to the dividend yield, does result in a still compelling yield to reset (assuming no redemption) approaching or, depending on one's assumptions, slightly exceeding 10% on an annualized basis. However, whether this is compelling enough to hold a preferred share whose rate may be subsequently fixed for a five-year period potentially below 7.0% is the core issue. Our perspective is that there are more compelling preferred shares available with superior long-term return potential and without the potential anchor of a below-market reset premium, especially when the rate will be fixed for the following five years.

Conclusion

The preferred shares at the current market quotation of around $22.75 are approaching a reasonable valuation based on the current yield and the potential reset rate yield, which will become fixed for a five-year period beginning in September 2026 (assuming the preferred shares are not redeemed at that time). We consider the likelihood of redemption to be modest at best based on our view that Western Alliance would likely not be able to issue new preferred shares with significantly better terms than the terms applicable once the rate resets. In the event forward interest rate expectations decline further over the course of the next year (which does not seem improbable), redemption would appear even less likely, while an increase in rates, which may encourage redemption, appears unlikely.

We thus believe that the current discount to the redemption value will be slow to accrue into the market price of the shares, limiting return contribution from price appreciation in the short term, while the current yield until the first potential redemption date, at 4.7%, is well below comparable securities. We believe the current market price will likely rise marginally as the redemption/reset date approaches but will ultimately remain below the redemption price due to the uncertainty about the ultimate reset rate and the potential for the five-year treasury rate to continue to decline over the course of the year, further depressing the ultimate reset rate.

In light of these considerations, we have sold all of our positions in Western Alliance preferred shares in favor of other opportunities with better long-term appreciation and yield potential.
