data.title: Broadcom Is A Linchpin Of The AI Infrastructure Ecosystem
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Broadcom (NASDAQ: AVGO) is firing on all cylinders at the epicenter of the AI boom. It is leveraging its unique position in custom silicon and networking to deliver explosive growth and robust profitability. Despite a premium valuation by traditional metrics, AVGO's data backed growth story underpinned by a colossal AI order backlog, high margin software revenue and disciplined execution. These factors convinces me why AVGO warrants a Buy rating. I remain confident that Broadcom's strategic focus on a few heavyweight AI customers, combined with its best in class networking chips and steady software cash flows will drive outsized shareholder returns in the years ahead.

Business Overview

Broadcom Inc is a global technology conglomerate with two primary segments. First is semiconductors which contributes about 66% of revenues. And second is infrastructure software which contributes about 33% of revenues. On the semiconductor side, Broadcom has built an enviable portfolio of networking, chips, custom accelerators and connectivity silicon that form the backbone of modern data centers. It is important to mention that, Broadcom supplies custom AI chips [ASICs] to a very narrow group of customers. These are essentially a handful of hyperscalers and AI labs racing to build LLMs. And this concentrated focus is by design. By zeroing to a small set of AI giants, the company ensures that it captures outsized value from those driving the bulk of AI infrastructure spend.

When I look at AVGO's strategy, I note that the company is focused in partnering deeply with customers to develop custom silicon solutions tailored to their massive workloads. This includes companies like Google (GOOG)(GOOGL) on its TPU AI accelerators and other major AI players. In fact, AVGO had 3 big AI customers a year ago and now has 5. AVGO's Tomahawk Ethernet switch family and related chips are also crucial for scaling AI networks. The latest Tomahawk 6 was unveiled as the world's first 102.4 Tbp switch chip. Its bandwidth enablers cluster sizes of up to 100K GPUs/accelerators.

On the software side, AVGO's acquisition of VMware valued its infrastructure software revenues to $27Bn in 2025. VMware's cloud and virtualization software is a steady, cash generative business that AVGO is using to complement its hardware dominance. Management has emphasized that the software segment if focused on durable, single digit growth with excellent margins. This provides a stable counterbalance to the volatile semiconductor cycle. VMware's flagship cloud foundation product saw strong adoption, helping infrastructure software grow by 26% y/y to $27B. While some skeptics feared Broadcom's notorious cost cutting could erode VMware's franchise, so far the integration appears on track.

Growth Drivers

Broadcom's opportunity can be summarized in a single word. AI. The world's pursuit of AI at scale is translating directly into years of demand for AVGO chips. A striking data point from the recent Q4 2025 is that AVGO's AI order backlog now exceeds $73Bn which represents almost half of the company's total backlog. What interests me more is that management expects $73Bn in AI orders to be delivered in the next 18 months. This means that AVGO booked nearly $75B of AI centric revenue through mid 2027 giving it an enviable growth runway.

Diving into this backlog, it spans Custom AI accelerators as well as the networking gear needed to connect them. Broadcom disclosed that just the order backlog for AI networking switches is over $10Bn. The latest Tomahawk 6 switch is the only chip of its kind on the market that is booking orders are record rates. This is as data centers upgrade to Ethernet based AI fabrics. Additionally, AVGO has secured record orders for the optical components and PCle connectivity chips that accompany these AI systems. This end to end involvement from custom compute engines to the plumbing around them means that AVGO captures a significant share of AI system value.

Secondly, Broadcom is expanding its customer base in AI. As I mentioned, AVGO recently added a 5th major XPU customer. This is a new $1Bn order from yet another customer due for delivery in 2026. In Q3, it was revealed that Anthropic (ANTHRO) was behind a surprise $10b order for AVGO's latest TPU based racks. ANTHRO then upped the ante with another $11Bn order in Q4. These are staggering commitments from a single new customer validating AVGO's value proposition to cutting edge AI companies. It is important to mention that AVGO'S top 4 AI customers also continue to increase their orders. The $73B backlog figure was achieved despite AVGO shipping record products this year. And this implies that new orders kept replenishing the queue.

When I look into the future, Broadcom's CEO is betting big on AI's trajectory. Hock Tan's new contract extension through 2030 includes performance incentives tied solely to AI revenue. For instance, AVGO must hit over $120Bn in AI revenue by 2030 for Tan to max out his bonus. To put this into perspective, AVGO's AI revenue in 2025 was $20B. So Tan is essentially targeting a 6x increase in 5 years. This ambitious goal speaks volume about management's confidence in the sustainability of AI demand. While 20230 is a long way off, it is telling that AVGO sees a path to +$100Bn in annual AI sales. This is a figure that would likely make it the second largest semiconductor player in AI after Nvidia (NVDA). As Tan noted during the Goldman conference," demand for compute is accelerating in the race towards generative superintelligence." And Broadcom intends to supply that compute.

Beyond AI, the VMware integration offers additional upside. AVGO is notorious for improving operating efficiency of acquisitions. With VMware, it definitely has a chance to expand margins and cross sell networking and security software info VMware's huge company customer base. And early indications are positive. Broadcom has refrained from extreme price hikes and is focusing on subscriptions. This aims for a smooth transition. And if AVGO can steadily grow VMware's revenues while cutting costs, then the earnings accretion will be quite significant. This ideally funds Broadcom's AI ambitions with stable cash flows, a strategy that the CEO has been executing for years.

Valuation

As I earlier said, Broadcom is no longer a bargain it was a couple of years ago. Of course the market has woken up to its AI leverage. The stock price has returned about 57% YTD. And Broadcom now trades at premium valuation relative to the average tech stock.

However, I'd argue that this premium is amply justified by Broadcom's growth. And relative to peers in the semiconductor arena, AVGO's valuation is reasonable on a growth adjusted basis.

To put things into perspective, let me compare Broadcom's key valuation metrics to two other AI chip players, Nvidia (NVDA) and AMD(AMD) and to the sector median.

AVGO NVDA AMD Sector Median
P/E TTM (GAAP) 71X 45X 111X 31X
FWD Non-GAAP PEG 1.04X 1.03X 1.19X 1.72X
FWD EV/EBITDA 25X 32X 49X 15X

When I look at the table above, AVGO is clearly more expensive than the typical tech stock trading at 71x TTM GAAP P/E compared to 31x for the sector. Its EV/EBITDA is 25x while the sector median is at 15x. However, when comparing to other chip makers AVGO's multiples are in a similar ballpark. When I factor in growth, Broadcom's PEG is below the sector median implying that the stock price is not outpacing earnings growth prospects.

It is also worth noting Broadcom's superior profitability in the mix. With about 68% EBITDA margins and 41% FCFs, AVGO justifies a higher multiple. And this is because it converts more of its revenue into cash than Nvidia or AMD. AVGO's ROE and capital are likewise exceptional When I adjust for these quality factors, AVGO's valuation looks much more palatable.

Broadcom's Key Risk

The biggest risk I see for AVGO is AI cycle dependency. Broadcom's recent surge is largely tied to the AI investment cycle. If AI spending by cloud providers slows, AVGO's growth could disappoint. The $73Bn backlog provides short term insulation but a sharp drop off in new orders beyond that horizon would be a bearish scenario.

Secondly, Broadcom suppers from customer concentration risk. AVGO's strategy of focusing on 5 to 7 key AI customers is a double edged sword. Those few relationships account for a huge portion of future revenue. If one of the Mag 7 were to falter, switch to an inhouse solution or choose a competitor for networking and connectivity, it would hurt Broadcom badly.

Final Thoughts

Broadcom has established itself as a linchpin of the AI Infrastructure ecosystem. My conviction remains stronger than ever. The company's blend of muscular growth and muscular profits is exceedingly rare. Yes, for sure the stock is not that cheap on the surface. I believe Broadcom's premium valuation is a price worth paying for its unique strategic position and execution. I think the market continues to underestimate how effectively Broadcom can ride the AI wave. It doesn't make flashy GPUs, but it makes almost everything else that enables AI at scale. And does so with a razor focus on the biggest customers.

data.title: WesBanco Preferred: An Appealing Series B Preferred
data.description:
WesBanco (WSBC) is a $17 billion bank holding company primarily serving West Virginia with additional operations in the surrounding region. In March 2023 we wrote positively about the company’s Series “A” preferred shares (WSBCP), which at the time offered an attractive (insofar as fixed income opportunities) 11.5% compound annual return through the expected preferred call date of November 2025. The basis for our expectation that the preferred shares would be called for redemption on the first possible opportunity was the significant reset premium incorporated into the terms of the Series “A” preferred issue – 6.557% over the then applicable five-year U.S. Treasury bond rate – which would then be fixed for the following five-year period.

Indeed, with five-year treasury rates at roughly 4.4% at the beginning of 2025, this implied a potential dividend reset rate of nearly 11% – far above any comparable bank preferred issue available at the time. Moreover, even if rates were to fall precipitously in 2025, which appeared unlikely at the time, the yield on the premium alone was equivalent to or only slightly less than other available bank preferred issues. Ultimately, interest rate cuts and other factors during 2025 only brought the five-year treasury bond rate down to around 3.6% shortly before the initial call date. The resulting prospective 10% yield virtually assured the preferred shares would be called by the company at redemption value or, at a minimum, that investors would hold a preferred stock trading at redemption value with a superior current income yield. This would have been similar to recently redeemed preferred shares of Customers Bancorp (CUBI-E and CUBI-F), which remained outstanding with high dividend yields for a period of time after the initial redemption date.

Thus, as expected, the Series “A” preferred shares were called for redemption by WesBanco and redeemed in full on November 15, 2025. The redemption was financed in large part by WesBanco’s issuance of a new series of quite similar Series “B” preferred shares (WSBCO), which have a higher initial fixed yield although a lower reset rate premium. The higher initial yield, from an income standpoint, makes the new preferred shares a still appealing option for current income oriented investors, especially those seeking tax advantaged qualified dividend distributions, although the reset rate premium is generally typical of recently issued preferred shares from other similar financial institutions and is thus itself unremarkable.

The Series “B” Preferred Shares

The WesBanco Series “B” preferred stock (WSBCO) is a series of non-cumulative reset rate preferred stock with a liquidation/redemption price of $25.00 per share. The preferred stock yields a fixed 7.375% of the stated value, payable quarterly, until October 1, 2030, at which time the dividend rate is reset every five years at a premium of 3.795% over the then applicable rate on the five-year U.S. Treasury bond.

The preferred shares are redeemable at the company’s option at the redemption price on October 1, 2030, and on October 1 every five years after the initial potential redemption date. In addition, the preferred shares are subject to potential redemption, as with many bank preferred shares, in the event that regulatory changes no longer permit the preferred shares to be included as a component of Tier 1 capital for regulatory purposes.

The preferred shares were originally issued on September 10, 2025, and the reset rate premium is generally reflective of similar issuances by financial institutions.

Issuer Strength

The attractiveness of a bank’s preferred securities is directly related to the attractiveness of the bank’s core operations. In assessing the strength of the underlying issue, we consider financial condition, operating results, asset/liability composition, the nature of the institution’s deposit, and capitalization ratios adjusted to include unrealized losses on investment securities. A thorough review of WesBanco is not within the scope of this article, but, in our assessment of the bank, we find that the issuer, on balance, has sufficient strength to support the preferred shares.

However, WesBanco’s recent results have been colored somewhat by the company’s relatively recent acquisition of Premier Financial Corporation, which makes discerning the underlying performance of the institution marginally more challenging since substantial increases in deposits, loans, etc., are largely reflecting the acquisition rather than the underlying strength of the core banking business.

Nonetheless, the company’s operating metrics remain compelling and certainly supportive of the preferred shares regardless of the merits of the company’s common stock. Organic growth within the company’s core market has remained consistent with prior trends, and the temporary impact of merger expenses on the bottom line will abate in the coming year.

WesBanco is also focused on driving expense reductions in order to leverage benefits from the consolidated entity. WesBanco has already announced the consolidation and/or closure of some 27 branches over the next year with a targeted efficiency ratio, a measure of a bank’s profitability, in the mid-50% range. This efficiency is certainly not the lowest we have seen nor is it the highest, and it falls notably in the middle of a range reasonable for decently performing larger community or small regional banks.

WesBanco's financial position from an institutional risk perspective is also modestly to significantly better than many comparable financial institutions. The company’s regulatory capital ratios are all well above those required to be well capitalized and, importantly, are not subject by our calculations to significant erosion in the event unrealized losses not otherwise reflected in shareholder’s equity are passed through the income statement to the balance sheet.

In addition, , per the company’s filings with the FFIEC, uninsured deposits continue to represent a relatively modest 33% of total deposits, more in line with smaller community banks and well below the 45% to 60% range common among larger regional banks.

Finally, with a market capitalization of approximately $3.3 billion, WesBanco could be an appealing potential acquisition for a number of institutions looking to expand into the region. WesBanco is among the top three deposit market share holders in West Virginia, a common indicator of acquisition appeal, with the majority of offices within the state. Recent market trends towards additional consolidation within the banking industry would not put an acquisition of the company beyond question. We’re certainly not counting on such an outcome – or predicting such – but an acquisition by a larger institution would potentially add additional financial strength backing the company’s preferred shares.

Conclusion

WesBanco’s Series “B” preferred shares are certainly not as compelling as the Series “A” preferred shares were at the time of our initial article amidst financial industry panic in the aftermath of the failure of Silicon Valley Bank. We certainly do not expect the 11%-12% compound annual returns available at that time in the Series “A” which were the result of depressed valuation of the preferred shares at that time, although we’d argue the Series “B” preferred shares warrant watching should turmoil again indiscriminately impact banking institution valuations. Nonetheless, at a current yield of approximately 7.2% and a yield to maturity of approximately 7.0%, assuming redemption on the first possible redemption date, conservative income-oriented investors could certainly do worse than the WesBanco Series “B” preferred.

data.title: Galderma: L'Oreal Is Doubling Down, But The Risk-Reward Looks Skewed To The Downside
data.description:
Thesis

On December 8th, L'Oréal S.A. (LRLCF) announced it is raising its stake in Galderma Group AG (GALDY) from 10% to 20%. We wanted to check the value behind the move. What we have found is a balance of risks we do not like since it's lofty valuation, high level of embedded growth in the stock price, and a new prescription medicine with wide and serious side effects and effectiveness that to us does not seem to be much higher than placebo.

But, this is not the first time L'Oréal has been buying stakes in Galderma, so let us start with their history overview.

History

Galderma was founded in 1981 as a joint venture of L’Oréal and Nestlé S.A. (NSRGY). Over time, it became relevant in medical dermatology and the development of cosmetic/dermatological products. In 2014, L’Oréal sells its entire stake to Nestlé and it becomes a part of its Nestlé Skin Health segment. Deal was a part of much bigger transaction involving Nestlé stake in L’Oréal. More on that deal can be found here.

Next, Nestlé sold the entire Galderma in 2019 to a consortium of institutional investors led by EQT private equity. In March 2024, EQT made an IPO of Galderma shares on SIX Swiss Exchange at a price of CHF 53 per share. On August 2024, L’Oréal announced it had acquired a 10% stake in Galderma valued at EUR 1.8 billion or about CHF 1.7 billion (note that exchange rate is about same today and at the moment of transaction.

That valued the entire company at CHF 17 billion and with current or CHF 75 per share at the time stock price was trading at CHF 72.5 so there was not a large premium. Now, the entire market cap of the company is about CHF 39.3 billion, we can say that L’Oréal made a great investment at that time.

New 10% stake will be bought from same private equity that sold the frist 10%. Price is undisclosed, but if one can judge by a previous deal we can expect it to be around market price of CHF 165 or $ 41.5 in ADR terms. Here, please note that every share has 5 ADRs.

MOAT

So, what is Galderma and what does it do?

It has three major segments: aesthetics, prescription dermatology, and consumer care.

Aesthetics is the segment that includes injectable aesthetic treatments such as fillers and botulinum products used by medical professionals for corrective and anti aging procedures. Prescription Dermatology (RX) is the segment focused on prescription medicines for acne, rosacea, and other chronic skin diseases. Consumer Care (Dermocosmetics) is the dermocosmetic segment that includes everyday skincare products such as Cetaphil.

Galderma’s aesthetics segment grows because demand for fillers and botulinum products is structurally rising. Clinicians rarely switch products once they are trained on a specific injectable so retention rates are very high. Restylane, Dysport and Sculptra provide stable recurring revenue through frequent treatments. The top competitor here is AbbVie Inc (ABBV) with the famous Botox and sales of the segment of $5.2 billion according to its annual report. The entire market for facial injectables is estimated to be valued at $9.54 billion with an estimated growth rate of 7.7%. Galdermas' segment sales is about $2.3 billion.

The RX segment targets chronic skin diseases that require long term therapy so demand is stable here. Differin, Epiduo, and rosacea treatments have strong clinical support, which makes it hard for competitors to displace them. Entry barriers are high due to regulation and the need for proven clinical efficacy. Main competitors here are companies like Amgen Inc (AMGN) with its Otezla for psoriasis and psoriatic arthritis. The entire market of derma pharma is estimated to $45.7 billion in 2025 with expected CAGR of 8.1%-9.5% to 2030. Galderma here is relatively small player with sales in the segment of $1.3 billion for 2024. According to last Q3 2025 conference call, Nemluvio brand is driving significant growth in dermatology.

Nemluvio is a prescription medicine used to treat atopic dermatitis and prurigo nodularis. Here, we need to acknowledge our limits in understanding the medicine, but the casual visit to the site got us surprised to a level we did not expect.

First, there is the indication where they note that it is not known if the drug is safe for certain ages.

Than, there is this note. Significant improvement is in 9% of cases vs 3% placebo. For us, that does not seem to be a high level of certainty in the product.

Plus, the real scare for us as investors and users is the safety information on the drug website.

So, if we take everything into account, we would argue that the product carries meaningful safety concerns, while its clinical effectiveness appears only marginally better than placebo. We see this as a central risk to earnings growth rates that are highly dependent on this single product.

The final segment is Consumer Care. Here, one of the largest competitors is L’Oréal itself, with CeraVe and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) with Neutrogena, and Beiersdorf Aktiengesellschaft (BDRFF) with Eucerin. Cetaphil relies on its reputation as a safe and dependable product for sensitive skin, with segment sales of $780 million, which is just a small player compared to all the stated heavyweights.

If we were to take everything into account, we would provide Galderma with wide moat. Moat derives from its two largest segments, aesthetics and prescription dermatology. Both of the segments have high barriers to entry due to the specific process it takes for prescription products to enter the market. As noted, clinicians rarely switch products once trained in a particular injectable, so retention rates are very high. There is also a certain brand value attached to the products that serves as a reinforcement of the moat. On the last and smallest segment, we do not believe there is any kind of moat since the company competes with much larger companies where brand awareness is the name of the game. However, please note that the wide moat does not eliminate reputational or financial risks that could be derived from Nemluvio. It is just another part of the scale that can be tipped at any moment.

Financial position

So, what is the financial position of Galderma?

To assess credit rating, we use the net interest coverage ratio with data provided by Professor Damodaran from NYU Stern. Most sources use the formula for the interest coverage ratio as EBIT divided by interest cost. We think that it is fair toward the company to use net interest cost due to the current interest rate environment. With EBIT of $747 million and net interest cost of $200 million, we calculate an interest coverage ratio of x3.74. We use scale for smaller and riskier companies since it competes with much larger companies.

The stated ratio gives the company an BB+ credit rating. The company has a current credit rating by S&P Global Inc. (SPGI) at a BBB rating with a positive outlook. We also note that the Nemluvio launch was hailed positively, while no risks were acknowledged.

So, in case of materialisation of any kind of side effects on a larger scale, we would argue for a risk of credit downgrade.

Reverse valuation

So what do investors pay when buying Galderma stock, and what has L’Oréal actually paid, if we assume it is the market price, since no more details are provided? Seeking Alpha valuation grade of it at the level of D-. Trailing PE is at the level of x128. Since market consensus is based on only one estimate we will ignore it.

We are using the FCFE model. Please note that we ignore all changes in working capital and debt levels. This is to focus on the core business, and due to our opinion that changes in working capital and debt levels shouldn't have a meaningful impact on valuation levels.

At the time of writing this article, the price of shares is around $41.18, RFR is 4.2% (US 10-year treasury), and the 5-year beta is 0.90. Please note that since we are doing reverse valuation in USD terms, we use RFR in USD terms, not in CHF terms. Also, just to clarify that our logic in using a perpetual growth rate equal to the RFR is to mitigate large changes in share price due to slight changes in the RFR.

For the 2025 FY earnings, we use the updated guidance we stated before. We use sales growth of 17% that gives us sales of $5.160 million, EBITDA margin of 23,1% that gives us $1.192 million with EBITDA growth of 34% YoY. We use this 34% growth as an estimate for full-year earnings growth. We project D&A and CapEx to grow in line with earnings. However, to justify the stated price, the company would actually need to grow its earnings by a rate of 12.6% in the period through 2027-2029. After that, it would need to grow over 10 years at 10.08%, going up to the level of the risk-free rate every year.

Here we will note again that a large part of embedded earnings growth comes from Nemluvio while risks stated clearly by the company tend to be ignored. Taking the current multiple into account compared to other peers and stated embedded double-digit growth rates, in the manner of a central bank governor, we find a balance of risks being tilted to the downside so we initiate with SELL.

Risks to bearish thesis

The main risks to our bearish thesis are Nemluvio becoming a real blockbuster with no real side effects. This is a science-driven company, and it can develop new products that can become blockbusters too. We see it as our responsibility to inform investors of the risks, either the ones that are behind FOMO and are tilted to the upside or the ones we stated in the article and are tilted to the downside.

To wrap it up

Galderma is one of those companies that has a lot of history with different owners who seemed always liked the company but were never quite sure what to do with it exactly. There were a lot of strategies written for Galderma by founding companies L’Oréal and Nestlé, but none of them seemed to work since both of the companies at some point decided to exit. Now, L’Oréal is back and raising the stakes again. Galderma stocks are trading in high multiples with high levels of growth priced in, as our reverse valuation shows. The majority of growth comes from Nemluvio, the new drug with serious and wide side effects, and efficiency slightly better than a placebo. We don't like this balance of risks and initiate with Sell.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
